coredata.dc:title: Enhanced reactive oxygen species activation for building carbon quantum dots modified Bi5O7I nanorod composites and optimized visible-light-response photocatalytic performance
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Rong
Chen, Zhigang
Ji, Mengxia
Chen, Hanxiang
Liu, Yiling
Xia, Jiexiang
Li, Huaming
coredata.dc:description:
Novel Carbon quantum dots modified Bi5O7I (CQDs/Bi5O7I) nanorod composites were prepared via an ionic liquid 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium iodide ([Emim]I) assisted solvothermal method for the first time. Series of characterization methods were used to explore the structural composition, morphology and optical properties of as-prepared CQDs/Bi5O7I photocatalysts. Compared with pure Bi5O7I nanorod, CQDs/Bi5O7I composites exhibited the increased the specific surface area and visible light capture capability so as to expose more active sites and make full use of sunlight. Meanwhile, the tight contact between CQDs and Bi5O7I was beneficial for the rapid separation and migration of photogenerated electron-hole pairs to further suppress the high recombination rate in the pure Bi5O7I nanorods. More generated superoxide radicals (O2
−) was detected by electron spin resonance analysis. In addition, the possible mechanism for the enhanced photocatalytic performance of CQDs/Bi5O7I composites was put forward.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bi5O7I
CQDs
Visible light
Photodegradation
Ionic liquid

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns of pure Bi5O7I and CQDs/Bi5O7I samples with different CQDs contents, (b) enlargement of XRD patterns.
FT-IR spectra of pure Bi5O7I and CQDs/Bi5O7I samples.
Raman spectra of pure Bi5O7I and 0.5 wt% CQDs/Bi5O7I samples.
XPS spectra of Bi5O7I and 0.5 wt% CQDs/Bi5O7I samples. (a) Survey of the samples; (b) Bi 4f; (c) O 1s; (d) I 3d; (e) C 1s. (f) Peak fitting of C 1s spectra of 0.5 wt% CQDs/Bi5O7I.
(a) UV–vis spectra of Bi5O7I and CQDs/Bi5O7I materials, (b) (αEphoton)1/2 vs. Ephoton curves of the as-prepared Bi5O7I sample.
TEM images of (a) Bi5O7I and (c) 0.5 wt% CQDs/Bi5O7I samples, high magnification TEM image of (b) CQDs and (d) 0.5 wt% CQDs/Bi5O7I materials. (e) EDS of the 0.5 wt% CQDs/Bi5O7I materials.
Nitrogen absorption-desorption isotherms of (a) pure Bi5O7I and (b) 0.5 wt% CQDs/Bi5O7I hybrid material.
Photocatalytic degradation of (a) BPA, (b) TC and (c) CIP in the presence of Bi5O7I and CQDs/Bi5O7I with different loading amounts under visible light irradiation.
(a) Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and (b) transient photocurrent response of pure Bi5O7I and 0.5 wt% CQDs/Bi5O7I materials.
Steady-state PL spectra of pure Bi5O7I and 0.5 wt% CQDs/Bi5O7I materials.
DMPO spin-trapping ESR spectra of Bi5O7I and 0.5 wt% CQDs/Bi5O7I in (a) methanol dispersion and (b) aqueous dispersion under visible light irradiation.
Free radicals trapping experiment toward BPA of 0.5 wt% CQDs/Bi5O7I under visible light irradiation.

(a) Cycling runs for the photodegradation of RhB in the presence of 0.5 wt% CQDs/Bi5O7I materials under visible light irradiation, (b) XRD patterns of the 0.5 wt% CQDs/Bi5O7I materials before and after the photocatalytic cycle experiments.
Valence-band XPS spectra of the 0.5 wt% CQDs/Bi5O7I and Bi5O7I materials.
Proposed charge transfer mechanism in the CQDs/Bi5O7I composites combined with the possible photocatalytic degradation mechanism.

head.ce:title: Enhanced reactive oxygen species activation for building carbon quantum dots modified Bi5O7I nanorod composites and optimized visible-light-response photocatalytic performance

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of the Environment and Safety Engineering, Jiangsu University, 301 Xuefu Road, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of the Environment and Safety Engineering

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental Science and Engineering
Nanjing University of Information Science & Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Novel Carbon quantum dots modified Bi5O7I (CQDs/Bi5O7I) nanorod composites were prepared via an ionic liquid assisted solvothermal method for the first time. The introduction of CQDs increased the specific surface area, visible light capture capability and separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs for CQDs/Bi5O7I composites. Holes and superoxide radicals were main active species in the photocatalytic degradation reaction.

Novel Carbon quantum dots modified Bi5O7I (CQDs/Bi5O7I) nanorod composites were prepared via an ionic liquid 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium iodide ([Emim]I) assisted solvothermal method for the first time. Series of characterization methods were used to explore the structural composition, morphology and optical properties of as-prepared CQDs/Bi5O7I photocatalysts. Compared with pure Bi5O7I nanorod, CQDs/Bi5O7I composites exhibited the increased the specific surface area and visible light capture capability so as to expose more active sites and make full use of sunlight. Meanwhile, the tight contact between CQDs and Bi5O7I was beneficial for the rapid separation and migration of photogenerated electron-hole pairs to further suppress the high recombination rate in the pure Bi5O7I nanorods. More generated superoxide radicals (O2
−) was detected by electron spin resonance analysis. In addition, the possible mechanism for the enhanced photocatalytic performance of CQDs/Bi5O7I composites was put forward.

ce:section.ce:para: Bisphenol A (BPA) is one of the most widely used industrial compounds in the world and mainly used for the production of plastics. However, BPA as a common endocrine disruptor cannot be degraded naturally. Nowadays, the efficient removal of new environmental pollutant endocrine disruptor has gradually become a hot research in the world [1–3]. Different new technologies have been developed to deal with these issues, such as photoelectrocatalysis [4–7], adsorption [8,9] and photocatalysis [10]. Among them, photocatalytic technology as a green and sustainable technology, not only has a wide range selectivity of pollutant, but also can effectively mineralize organic pollutants to be H2O and CO2 [11,12]. However, the traditional photocatalysts like TiO2 only can be excited by UV light, that greatly limit their applications in visible light even the near infrared region. Therefore, the core of photocatalytic technology is to find efficient visible-light response photocatalysts [13].

Bismuth oxyhalides, BiOX (X = Cl, Br and I) as a class of promising photocatalysts, have attracted people’s attention due to their unique layered structure which composed of [Bi2O2]2+ and double [X]− layer [14–19]. The electrostatic field between layers can effectively induce the separation of photogenerated charge carriers boosting the increased photocatalytic activity [20]. Therefore, BiOX materials can be effectively used to degrade organic pollutants in the environment. Among these compounds, bismuth oxyiodide (BiOI) has received extensive attention for its strongest optical absorption property in the visible light range [21]. However, the high valence band (VB) position and the rapid recombination of photogenerated electron-holes of BiOI greatly limit its photocatalytic activity so that it cannot be applied to practical applications. Recently, many effective strategies have been applied to improve the photocatalytic performance of BiOI, for example, bismuth-rich strategy [22], facet ratio control [23], morphology control [24] and surface modification [25]. In 2009, Wang’s group synthesized Bi5O7I material for the first time and applied it in the photodegradation of rhodamine B (RhB) under visible light irradiation [26]. Recently, Ye’s group reported that Bi5O7I with different dominant facets synthesized by molecular precursor hydrolysis and calcination showed facet-dependent photocatalytic activity for N2 fixation and RhB removal [27]. However, Bi5O7I nanomaterial still has some drawbacks, such as defective light absorption capability, low migration and high recombination efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, which limit its visible-light-response photocatalytic activity. Zhang’s group prepared g-C3N4/Bi5O7I heterojunction photocatalyst, which showed much higher photocatalytic activity for degradation of RhB and phenol under visible light than pure g-C3N4 and Bi5O7I [28]. However, few researches have been investigated on construction of Bi5O7I-based photocatalysts to improve the visible-light absorption ability and charge carriers’ separation efficiency of Bi5O7I material.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a new type of carbon-related materials, are composed of carbon skeleton with the diameter less than 10 nm and some surface groups [29,30]. Benefiting from its non-toxic, bandgap tenability, high biocompatibility, strong light harvesting capability in the visible even near-infrared region and favorable electronic storage performance, CQDs have been successfully applied in the different research fields of biological imaging, fluorescence sensing and catalysis [31,32]. Very recently, CQDs have been introduced into the photocatalysis field owing to the superior electron transfer ability and photon absorption capability. Series of CQDs/semiconductor composites with excellent photocatalytic activity have been reported for application in degradation of organic pollutants, such as CQDs/TiO2 [33], CQDs/g-C3N4 [34], CQDs/Ag3PO4 [35], etc. It can be anticipated after the introduction of CQDs into Bi5O7I sample, the extended absorption in visible-light region of CQDs/Bi5O7I composite could be obtained. However, the key role of CQDs in enhancing photocatalytic activity remains to be further investigated.

In this work, a series of CQDs/Bi5O7I nanorod photocatalysts have been firstly synthesized through ionic liquid [Emim]I assisted hydrothermal method. Ionic liquid acted as I source and dispersing agent during solvothermal process at the same time. According to our preliminary researches, the introduction of CQDs could enhance the visible light absorption capacity and promote the rapid separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs of the photocatalysts. The photocatalytic activity of CQDs/Bi5O7I composites was evaluated by the degradation of BPA, tetracycline hydrochloride (TC), ciprofloxacin (CIP) and RhB under visible light irradiation. Active species trapping experiments and electron spin resonance (ESR) analysis have been constructed to confirm the main active species produced during the photodegradation process. Furthermore, the possible mechanism for the enhanced photocatalytic activity was also discussed in detail.

1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium iodide ([Emim]I 99%) was purchased from Shanghai Chengjie Chemical Co. Ltd. All the other chemical reagents were of analytical grade which ordered from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd and used without further purification. The CQDs were synthesized by a simple hydrothermal method according to the previous literature and then handled by freeze-drying [31]. The CQDs modified Bi5O7I photocatalysts were prepared through a facile hydrothermal treatment. Typically, 1 mmol bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O) and a certain amount of CQDs was dispersed in 10 mL of mannitol solution (0.1 M) with continuous magnetic stirring and marked as solution A. 1 mmol [Emim]I was added into 8 mL mannitol solution to obtain solution B. Then solution B was added into solution A drop by drop under continuous stirring and added NaOH aqueous solution (1 M) to adjust the pH value to 13. After stirring for 0.5 h, the resulting suspension was transferred into Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclaves (25 mL) and kept at 140 °C for 24 h. The sludge was centrifuged and washed three times with distilled water and ethanol. At last, the product was dried overnight at 60 °C under air atmosphere. In CQDs/Bi5O7I materials, the introducted amounts of CQDs were 0.2, 0.5 and 1 wt%, respectively. Similarly, Bi5O7I material was obtained through the same method without adding CQDs.

The crystalline structure of as-prepared photocatalysts was measured on a Shimadzu XRD-6000 X-ray diffractometer using Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.54 Å) and 2θ scan rate of 7 min−1. Elements chemical states were determined in ESCALAB MKII X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), using Mg Kα excitation source. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JSM-7001F, JEOL) was used to observe the microstructure and morphology of as-synthesized materials, which equipped with an energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscope (EDS) operated at an acceleration voltage of 10 kV, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) (JEM-2100, JEOL, Japan) operating at 200 kV. The DRS of the samples was measured using the UV-vis spectrophotometer (UV-2450, Shimadzu Corporation, Japan), with BaSO4 powder as the standard reference. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured on Nicolet Model Nexus 470 infrared spectrometer using KBr tablet. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the samples were determined on a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrometer with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The BET specific surface area of the sample was calculated from the low temperature N2 adsorption-desorption experiment data, which carried out on surface area analyzer (TriStar II 3020, Micromeritics Instrument Corporation, USA). The ESR signals of radicals spin-trapped were measured on a Bruker model ESR JES-FA 200 spectrometer by spin-trap reagent dimethyl pyridine N-oxide (DMPO) in water and methanol, respectively.

The photocatalytic activities of as-prepared samples were evaluated by the degradation of BPA, CIP, TC and RhB under visible light irradiation. In the Pyrex glass reaction bottle, 50 mg photocatalyst was dispersed in BPA (100 mL, 10 mg L−1), CIP (100 mL, 10 mg L−1), TC (100 mL, 20 mg L−1) aqueous solution and 30 mg photocatalyst in RhB aqueous solution (100 mL, 10 mg L−1), respectively. Experiments were conducted under 30 °C with a circulating water system to prevent thermal catalysis. The photoreactor was equipped with a 300 W Xenon lamp and a 400 nm UV cutoff filter to ensure visible light irradiation. In each test, the suspension was stirred in the dark for 30 min to achieve adsorption-desorption balance. Then turn on the Xenon lamp, at a certain time interval, 4 mL of the reaction solution was sampled and centrifuged for several minutes to remove the particles. The UV–vis spectrophotometer was used to measure the concentration changes of CIP, TC and RhB solution with the absorption wavelength at 276, 356 and 553 nm, respectively. The remaining amount of BPA was analyzed using high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) which possessed an Agilent TC-C (18) column, two Varian ProStar 210 pumps and a Varian ProStar 325 UV–vis Detector at wavelength of 230 nm. Among then, the mobile phase was a mixture of methanol and water (volume ratio 3:1), the flow rate was 1 mL/min and 20 μL of the reacted BPA solution was injected each time.

In this work, an electrochemical analyzer equipped with a standard three-electrode system was taken to detect the photoelectrochemical properties of as-prepared materials, in which a saturated Ag/AgCl electrode used as reference electrode, a platinum wire as counter electrode, and the indium-tin oxide (ITO) glass (0.5 × 1 cm2) covered with Bi5O7I or CQDs/Bi5O7I sample (0.1 mg) as working electrode, respectively. A 500 W Xenon lamp was utilized as the photosource and the current was 13 A. The photocurrent measurement was measured in 0.1 M phosphate buffered saline solution with pH = 7 while electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) carried out in a 0.1 M KCl solution containing 5 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/Fe(CN)6 4−, respectively.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) was used to investigate the phase structure of the Bi5O7I and CQDs/Bi5O7I samples. As illustrated in Fig. 1 a, all the diffraction peaks of the samples could be matched well with the XRD standard patterns (JCPDS No. 38-0669) and lattice constants of a = 18.387 Å, b = 4.250 Å, c = 13.254 Å. After CQDs was introduced, all the diffraction peaks were well-matched with Bi5O7I. However, no obvious diffraction peaks of CQDs could be detected, presumably due to its low amount in the CQDs/Bi5O7I materials. As observed in all samples, an enlarged pattern of the diffraction peaks in the range of 2θ = 27-33˚ was shown in Fig. 1b, which suggests that with the increase of CQDs content in the Bi5O7I nanorod, the peak position of Bi5O7I shifted slightly toward a lower 2θ value. The same results were also observed in the other diffraction peaks, which indicated CQDs have been doped into Bi5O7I nanomaterial successfully [36].

Fig. 2 showed the FT-IR spectra of as-prepared Bi5O7I and CQDs/Bi5O7I samples. The absorption band of these samples at 541 cm−1 was corresponding to the Bi-O stretching mode [37]. It can be found two new absorption peaks of different CQDs/Bi5O7I composites at 1460 cm−1 was belonged to the symmetric carboxylate stretching [38] and 1660 cm−1 was attributed to CO stretching [39]. In addition, as the doping amount of CQDs increased, the absorption peak intensity was enhanced accordingly, which indicated CQDs/Bi5O7I composites have been successfully prepared. In order to obtain better confirmation on the microstructure of as-synthesized Bi5O7I and CQDs/Bi5O7I materials, Raman spectra were also conducted and shown in Fig. 3 . Raman bands located at 100.3 and 151.4 cm−1 could be seen in pure Bi5O7I photocatalyst, which were attributed to the A1g and E1g internal Bi-I stretching modes [40–42], respectively. However, these two peaks displayed slight shift to 101.1 and 149.9 cm−1 for 0.5 wt% CQDs/Bi5O7I composite, respectively, which could be attributed to the strong interaction between CQDs and Bi5O7I.

The optical absorption properties of the Bi5O7I and CQDs/Bi5O7I with different CQDs contents were studied by DRS (Fig. 5 a). It can be intuitively seen that the optical absorption edge of pure Bi5O7I was 415 nm. Simultaneously, with the amount of CQDs increased, the optical response range from 415 to 800 nm of CQDs/Bi5O7I composites was gradually increased, which might be owing to the photon absorption capability of CQDs in Bi5O7I sample [46]. In addition, Fig. 5b showed the band gap of Bi5O7I photocatalyst, which was calculated to be 2.92 eV using the classical Tauc approach [47].

The morphologies of as-prepared Bi5O7I and different CQDs/Bi5O7I samples were observed by TEM and HR-TEM. As displayed in Fig. 6 a, the pure Bi5O7I had a rod-like structure and the smooth surface. It can be clearly seen some dark dots with diameter about 5 nm were distributed in Fig. 6b and Fig. S1a, the lattice spacing of 0.32 nm was corresponding to the (0 0 2) crystal plane of CQDs [48]. After modified with 0.2 wt% CQDs (Fig. S1b), no obvious change can be observed in 0.2 wt% CQDs/Bi5O7I. However, with the doping amount of CQDs increased, the surface of CQDs/Bi5O7I samples became roughly, as shown in Fig. 6c and Fig. S1c. It can be speculated the CQDs have partially doped into Bi5O7I nanorods. The lattice of CQDs can be observed in the HR-TEM image of 0.5 wt% CQDs/Bi5O7I sample (Fig. 6d). Meanwhile, lattice spacing at 0.238 and 0.317 nm was attributed to (6 0 2) and (3 1 1) plane of Bi5O7I, respectively. The obtained result further confirmed CQDs/Bi5O7I composite has been prepared successfully. Based on elemental mapping and EDS analysis results (Fig. 6e and Fig. S2), Bi, I, O and C elements could be found in CQDs/Bi5O7I composite and these elements were evenly distributed.
Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2018.07.027.

The morphologies of as-prepared Bi5O7I and different CQDs/Bi5O7I samples were observed by TEM and HR-TEM. As displayed in Fig. 6 a, the pure Bi5O7I had a rod-like structure and the smooth surface. It can be clearly seen some dark dots with diameter about 5 nm were distributed in Fig. 6b and Fig. S1a , the lattice spacing of 0.32 nm was corresponding to the (0 0 2) crystal plane of CQDs [48]. After modified with 0.2 wt% CQDs (Fig. S1b), no obvious change can be observed in 0.2 wt% CQDs/Bi5O7I. However, with the doping amount of CQDs increased, the surface of CQDs/Bi5O7I samples became roughly, as shown in Fig. 6c and Fig. S1c. It can be speculated the CQDs have partially doped into Bi5O7I nanorods. The lattice of CQDs can be observed in the HR-TEM image of 0.5 wt% CQDs/Bi5O7I sample (Fig. 6d). Meanwhile, lattice spacing at 0.238 and 0.317 nm was attributed to (6 0 2) and (3 1 1) plane of Bi5O7I, respectively. The obtained result further confirmed CQDs/Bi5O7I composite has been prepared successfully. Based on elemental mapping and EDS analysis results (Fig. 6e and Fig. S2), Bi, I, O and C elements could be found in CQDs/Bi5O7I composite and these elements were evenly distributed.

BET measurement was conducted to examine the specific surface area of Bi5O7I and CQDs/Bi5O7I photocatalysts. As shown in Fig. 7 and Fig. S3, the BET surface area of Bi5O7I, 0.2 wt% CQDs/Bi5O7I, 0.5 wt% CQDs/Bi5O7I and 1 wt% CQDs/Bi5O7I samples were calculated to be 9.70, 14.99, 17.76 and 16.71 m2 g−1, respectively. The reason for the increased specific surface area might be attributed to the rough surface of as-prepared CQDs/Bi5O7I composites after the introduction of CQDs, which was consistent with TEM analysis. As is known to all, a larger surface area is beneficial for photocatalysts to provide more adsorption and reaction sites for pollutants, which could bring about the enhanced photocatalytic performance [19]. Therefore, it can be forecasted CQDs/Bi5O7I composites may exhibit the improved photocatalytic performance for organic pollutant removal.

BET measurement was conducted to examine the specific surface area of Bi5O7I and CQDs/Bi5O7I photocatalysts. As shown in Fig. 7 and Fig. S3, the BET surface area of Bi5O7I, 0.2 wt% CQDs/Bi5O7I, 0.5 wt% CQDs/Bi5O7I and 1 wt% CQDs/Bi5O7I samples were calculated to be 9.70, 14.99, 17.76 and 16.71 m2 g−1, respectively. The reason for the increased specific surface area might be attributed to the rough surface of as-prepared CQDs/Bi5O7I composites after the introduction of CQDs, which was consistent with TEM analysis. As is known to all, a larger surface area is beneficial for photocatalysts to provide more adsorption and reaction sites for pollutants, which could bring about the enhanced photocatalytic performance [19]. Therefore, it can be forecasted CQDs/Bi5O7I composites may exhibit the improved photocatalytic performance for organic pollutant removal.

To appraise the photocatalytic activity of Bi5O7I and CQDs/Bi5O7I composites, photodegradation experiments were conducted by choosing BPA as target pollutant under the visible light illumination. Fig. 8 a displayed the photocatalytic degradation curves of BPA in the presence of Bi5O7I or CQDs/Bi5O7I with different loading amounts, respectively. The results showed that pure Bi5O7I exhibited poor photocatalytic activity, only 34.9% BPA was removed after visible light irradiated for 120 min. When the CQDs were introduced, CQDs/Bi5O7I photocatalysts displayed significantly enhanced photocatalytic performance. After irradiated for 120 min, 43%, 94.2% and 62.1% BPA were removed by 0.2, 0.5 and 1 wt% CQDs/Bi5O7I, respectively, in which the 0.5 wt% CQDs/Bi5O7I composite possessed the most outstanding photocatalytic activity. Fig. S4 displayed the details of the photodegradation of BPA by Bi5O7I and CQDs/Bi5O7I photocatalysts using HPLC Chromatography.

To appraise the photocatalytic activity of Bi5O7I and CQDs/Bi5O7I composites, photodegradation experiments were conducted by choosing BPA as target pollutant under the visible light illumination. Fig. 8 a displayed the photocatalytic degradation curves of BPA in the presence of Bi5O7I or CQDs/Bi5O7I with different loading amounts, respectively. The results showed that pure Bi5O7I exhibited poor photocatalytic activity, only 34.9% BPA was removed after visible light irradiated for 120 min. When the CQDs were introduced, CQDs/Bi5O7I photocatalysts displayed significantly enhanced photocatalytic performance. After irradiated for 120 min, 43%, 94.2% and 62.1% BPA were removed by 0.2, 0.5 and 1 wt% CQDs/Bi5O7I, respectively, in which the 0.5 wt% CQDs/Bi5O7I composite possessed the most outstanding photocatalytic activity. Fig. S4 displayed the details of the photodegradation of BPA by Bi5O7I and CQDs/Bi5O7I photocatalysts using HPLC Chromatography.

At the same time, antibiotics TC and CIP were also chosen to further evaluate the photocatalytic activity of as-prepared CQDs/Bi5O7I photocatalysts. It can be clearly seen from Fig. 8b and c, the introduction of CQDs could significantly improve the photocatalytic performance of Bi5O7I. In addition, 0.5 wt% CQDs/Bi5O7I exhibited the highest photocatalytic activity, about 30% and 40% increase for TC and CIP removal under visible light irradiation for 120 min compared to pure Bi5O7I material, respectively. RhB, as a common dye pollutant model, was also selected to assess the photocatalytic activity of as-prepared Bi5O7I-based photocatalysts. Fig. S5 displayed the 0.5 wt% CQDs/Bi5O7I composite also possessed the highest photocatalytic activity among other materials. The photocatalytic degradation experiments of four different pollutants manifested that the CQDs modified Bi5O7I material was a multi-selective and high-efficiency photocatalyst with broadband spectrum response. Moreover, the CQDs modification strategy is efficient to improve the photocatalytic activity of Bi5O7I nanorods for the degradation of different organic pollutants in the wastewater.

At the same time, antibiotics TC and CIP were also chosen to further evaluate the photocatalytic activity of as-prepared CQDs/Bi5O7I photocatalysts. It can be clearly seen from Fig. 8b and c, the introduction of CQDs could significantly improve the photocatalytic performance of Bi5O7I. In addition, 0.5 wt% CQDs/Bi5O7I exhibited the highest photocatalytic activity, about 30% and 40% increase for TC and CIP removal under visible light irradiation for 120 min compared to pure Bi5O7I material, respectively. RhB, as a common dye pollutant model, was also selected to assess the photocatalytic activity of as-prepared Bi5O7I-based photocatalysts. Fig. S5 displayed the 0.5 wt% CQDs/Bi5O7I composite also possessed the highest photocatalytic activity among other materials. The photocatalytic degradation experiments of four different pollutants manifested that the CQDs modified Bi5O7I material was a multi-selective and high-efficiency photocatalyst with broadband spectrum response. Moreover, the CQDs modification strategy is efficient to improve the photocatalytic activity of Bi5O7I nanorods for the degradation of different organic pollutants in the wastewater.

It is commonly known that the separation and transfer efficiency of photogenerated carriers greatly affect the photocatalytic activity of semiconductor photocatalysts. EIS and photocurrent test were employed to investigate the separation and recombination rate of photogenerated carriers in pure Bi5O7I and different CQDs/Bi5O7I composites. Generally speaking, the smaller the diameter of the Nyquist plot, the lower the resistance of charge transferring [43]. It can be seen that 0.5 wt% CQDs/Bi5O7I possessed the lowest electron-transfer resistance among these photocatalysts (Fig. 9 a). Also can be observed in Fig. 9b, 0.5 wt% CQDs/Bi5O7I material displayed the highest photocurrent intensity, almost eight times higher than that of pure Bi5O7I/ITO electrode. This significantly enhanced photocurrent intensity and decreased resistance value was ascribed to the introduced CQDs as electron acceptors facilitated the rapid separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs to further suppress the high recombination rate of Bi5O7I nanorods under visible light irradiaiton, thus resulting in the improved photocatalytic activity.

To learn more about the properties of charge transfer and recombination of as-prepared photocatalysts, steady-state PL analysis of Bi5O7I and 0.5 wt% CQDs/Bi5O7I composite materials was conducted, the results displayed in Fig. 10 . 0.5 wt% CQDs/Bi5O7I photocatalyst owned the lower PL intensity than pure Bi5O7I, suggesting the photogenerated electron-hole pairs of CQDs modified Bi5O7I possessed the lower recombination rate, which was in accordance with the results of EIS and photocurrent analysis.

For the purpose of confirming the active species generated in Bi5O7I and CQDs/Bi5O7I materials during the photodegradation process, the ESR was conducted. The presence of O2
− and
OH was detected in methanol and aqueous dispersion, respectively. Fig. 11 a showed that no signal in the ESR spectra could be detected in the dark. After visible light irradiated, the intensity of DMPO-O2
− of 0.5 wt% CQDs/Bi5O7I was much stronger than pure Bi5O7I, it could be inferred that the added CQDs allowed more electrons to participate in the reduction of O2. Abundant O2
− was produced by 0.5 wt% CQDs/Bi5O7I during the photodegradation process. From Fig. 11b, no remarkable change of the intensity of DMPO-
OH for pure Bi5O7I and 0.5 wt% CQDs/Bi5O7I can be seen, which revealed that
OH was not the main active specie in CQDs/Bi5O7I composites.

To further make sure the active species over CQDs/Bi5O7I photocatalyst during the photocatalysis process, free radicals trapping experiments were performed by adding different scavengers, N2 for O2
−, tert-butanol (T-butanol) and isopropyl alcohol (IPA) for
OH and triethanolamine (TEOA) for holes, respectively. As shown in Fig. 12 , the BPA degradation efficiency of 0.5 wt% CQDs/Bi5O7I was greatly inhibited in N2 or TEOA, respectively, which implied O2
− and holes were the main active species in the photocatalytic degradation reaction. Moreover, when 1 mmol T-butanol or IPA was added, there was no obvious change in photodegradation efficiency, which manifested that
OH was not the active species. The results were consistent with ESR analysis.

The structural stability of the semiconductor photocatalyst is an important measurement to evaluate the quality of photocatalysts. To evaluate the stability of CQDs/Bi5O7I photocatalyst, recirculation reaction was conducted for the photodegradation of RhB over 0.5 wt% CQDs/Bi5O7I composite under visible light irradiation. After four photocatalytic cycle experiments, the photodegradation efficiency still stayed around 79.4%, which implied the high stability of CQDs/Bi5O7I material (Fig. 13 a). Moreover, Fig. 13b displayed the XRD pattern of the 0.5 wt% CQDs/Bi5O7I material before and after photocatalytic cycle experiments. It could be intuitive to observe that the phase structures of the used CQDs/Bi5O7I material did not change, which revealed that CQDs/Bi5O7I material was fully stable during the photocatalytic process.

For the purpose of understanding the photo-induced carrier separation and transfer mechanism, the energy band structure involving band gap, VB and conduction band (CB) position of the Bi5O7I nanorod was confirmed by XPS valence spectra and DRS analysis. The electronic density of states can be determined by VB XPS spectra. As displayed in Fig. 14 , the VB edge of Bi5O7I was measured to be 1.36 eV. Combined with a correction value 0.63 eV for XPS valence spectra, VB value of Bi5O7I was calculated to be 1.99 eV. Based on the band gap, the CB potential of Bi5O7I was calculated to be -0.93 eV. Since the CB of Bi5O7I was negative than E0 (O2/O2
−) (−0.046 eV vs NHE) [49], electrons on the CB of Bi5O7I could reduce O2 to be O2
−. While the VB value (1.99 eV) was lower than E0 (
OH/OH−) (2.38 eV vs NHE) [50], which indicated Bi5O7I cannot oxidize OH− to generate
OH. For 0.5 wt% CQDs/Bi5O7I photocatalyst, VB value after corrected was 2.10 eV, which illustrated that the modification of CQDs had a slight influence on the electronic density of Bi5O7I.

According to the above experimental results, the photocatalytic mechanism of 0.5 wt% CQDs/Bi5O7I was put forward, as illustrated in Fig. 15 . Under the irradiation of visible light, electrons on the VB of Bi5O7I were excited and transferred to the CB, leaving the corresponding holes on the VB. The photogenerated electrons on the CB could be quickly transferred to the CQDs, causing effective separation of photogengrated electron-hole pairs and suppressing the recombination of electron-hole pairs. Then the transferred electrons accumulated on CQDs could effectively reduce more O2 to generated O2
− by one electron process. These abundant produced reactive oxygen species could instantly oxidize organic pollutants to form inorganic small molecules under visible light irradiation. In this system, CQDs in CQDs/Bi5O7I composites not only could enhance the absorption range in visible light region, but also enhanced the separation efficiency of electron-holes pairs and generation of abundant reactive oxygen species, bringing about the excellent visible-light-response photocatalytic degradation activity of organic pollutants.

In summary, novel CQDs/Bi5O7I nanorod composites have been firstly synthesized via a facile ionic liquid assisted solvothermal method. According to the reported literature, Bi5O7I showed poor photocatalytic activity for the degradation of colorless pollutants. In this study, as-prepared CQDs/Bi5O7I composites exhibited excellent structure stability and photocatalytic performance under visible light irradiation, especially for BPA degradation. The notable increase in photocatalytic performance of 0.5 wt% CQDs/Bi5O7I composite can be attributed to the improved charge separation efficiency and the enhanced light harvesting capability after the introduction of CQDs. The results of ESR analysis and free radicals trapping experiments demonstrated h+ and O2
− were the main active species in the photodegradation reaction, and more generated reactive oxygen radicals (O2
−) exhibited a decisive effect for boosting the increased photocatalytic performance. This work provides the ideas for full activation of reactive oxygen species in other CQDs modified semiconductor photocatalysts.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21471069, 21476098 and 21576123) and Jiangsu University Scientific Research Funding (No.11JDG0146).
